title: Cleanness (Purity)
summary: The repository XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item ACS0188.0001.001. U-M encoded the title page and 1,812-line Middle English poem from Robert J. Menner's 1920 edition Purity while retaining page milestones and images for the edition's largely untranscribed front and back matter. Menner edited the unique medieval witness, British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2, with reference to manuscript photographs and Morris's earlier editions. Printed editions, scans of those editions, the U-M page-image package, the physical manuscript, manuscript facsimiles, and Olsen's later diplomatic edition are modeled separately. The complete downloadable manuscript surrogate is microfilm-derived; Commons and the University of Calgary present materially different rights claims, so neither claim is transferred to the other provider or to British Library imaging orders.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for acs0188
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion, catalog, and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The XML's internal U-M identifier is ACS0188.0001.001 and its Library of Congress call number field is 828 C62 M55.
The source submodule is a distinct git repository; this manifest does not alter the XML.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item ACS0188.0001.001
entities.description: A public electronic representation of Menner's Middle English text, with linked images for the complete printed volume and legacy TEI/SGML-derived structural markup.

date_statements.label: header creation
date_statements.notes: The raw package's pageview support file is dated 2006-02-22; image-file timestamps are earlier and do not by themselves establish publication.

notes: The U-M header describes an uncorrected OCR text with no editing and TEI in Libraries Level 1 markup.
Continuous transcription is confined to the title page and Middle English poem; page milestones and images preserve access to Menner's other matter.

entities.label: Robert J. Menner edition, Purity, a Middle English Poem (1920)

bibliographic.citation: Robert J. Menner, Purity, a Middle English Poem, Yale Studies in English 61 (New Haven: Yale University Press; London: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1920).
bibliographic.title: Purity, a Middle English Poem
bibliographic.publisher: Yale University Press; Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press

notes: Introduction pp. vii–lxii; edited text pp. 1–66; notes pp. 67–178; glossary pp. 179–214; bibliography pp. 215–219; appendix pp. 221–234.
Menner selected Purity as his title instead of Cleanness or Clannesse.
The edition was prepared from manuscript rotographs or photographs and checked against the manuscript's offsets, corrections, and earlier editions.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Menner's 1920 Purity
entities.description: A Google-digitized PDF of the complete printed volume, uploaded to Internet Archive by ARLIMA in 2017.

entities.label: Richard Morris, Early English Alliterative Poems, first edition (1864)

bibliographic.citation: Richard Morris, Early English Alliterative Poems in the West-Midland Dialect of the Fourteenth Century, Early English Text Society Original Series 1 (London, 1864).
bibliographic.title: Early English Alliterative Poems in the West-Midland Dialect of the Fourteenth Century
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society Original Series 1

notes: This is the first complete printed edition of Cleanness identified in the sources reviewed; Menner abbreviates it M*.

entities.label: Richard Morris, Early English Alliterative Poems, second edition revised (1869)

bibliographic.citation: Richard Morris, Early English Alliterative Poems in the West-Midland Dialect of the Fourteenth Century, 2nd ed., revised, Early English Text Society Original Series 1 (London, 1869).
bibliographic.title: Early English Alliterative Poems in the West-Midland Dialect of the Fourteenth Century
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society Original Series 1

notes: Internet Archive metadata labels the scanned volume 1864, but the inspected title page explicitly reads ‘Second Edition, Revised, 1869.’ Menner abbreviates this revised edition M.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2
alternate_labels: Cotton MS Nero A X, article 3
entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Cleanness and also of Pearl, Patience, and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight. The Cleanness catalog component occupies modern folios 60r–86r, with prefatory drawings on 60r–60v and text on 61r–86r; corresponding older ink foliation is 56r–82r and 57r–82r.

historical_designations: Cotton MS Nero A X, article 3
Cotton MS Nero A X, ff. 41–130
holding.notes: The original composite Cotton MS Nero A X was separated into two volumes in 1964.

physical_description.extent: 90 folios in the separately bound volume; Cleanness at modern folios 60r–86r
physical_description.format: Parchment codex, written in one column of 36 lines
notes: Gothic cursive script described as textura rotunda with Anglicana features.
One scribe copied the manuscript in a South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire dialect; the poet's dialect is distinguished as farther south in Staffordshire.
Two drawings precede Cleanness and thirteen decorated initials occur within it; modern f. 86r contains the poem's final eleven lines and the first illustration for Patience.
Modern pencil foliation ff. 41–130 corresponds to older ink foliation ff. 37–126.

entities.label: Gollancz facsimile of Cotton Nero A X/2 (1923)

bibliographic.citation: Pearl, Cleanness, Patience and Sir Gawain, Reproduced in Facsimile from the Unique MS Cotton Nero A.x, introduction by Israel Gollancz, Early English Text Society Original Series 162 (London: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1923).
bibliographic.title: Pearl, Cleanness, Patience and Sir Gawain, Reproduced in Facsimile from the Unique MS Cotton Nero A.x
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society Original Series 162
bibliographic.publisher: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society

physical_description.extent: 44 pages of introduction, two leaves, and photographic facsimile of old-numbered folios 37–126
notes: The facsimile covers the complete manuscript now separately bound as Cotton MS Nero A X/2.

entities.label: Kenna L. Olsen diplomatic edition of Cleanness (2011)

bibliographic.citation: Kenna L. Olsen, Cleanness: A Diplomatic Edition with Textual, Codicological and Paleographical Notes, Publications of the Cotton Nero A.x Project 1 (Calgary, 2011).
bibliographic.title: Cleanness: A Diplomatic Edition with Textual, Codicological and Paleographical Notes
bibliographic.series: Publications of the Cotton Nero A.x Project 1

physical_description.format: Born-digital scholarly edition distributed in four PDF files
notes: Front matter, introduction and preface, diplomatic text, and bibliography are separate downloads.
Olsen revised her 2007 doctoral thesis after repeated manuscript study and examination of British Library high-resolution TIFFs supplied in 2011.
This later edition is a comparison source, not an upstream source of the U-M encoding.

editorial_practices.representation: Expanded silently except where a special or disputed expansion required comment
editorial_practices.description: Menner states that ordinary abbreviations are expanded without italics or comment; special cases and departures from earlier editions are discussed separately.
notes: Italic type in the electronic text should not be assumed to be a universal visual record of manuscript expansion marks.

editorial_practices.description: Menner says that manuscript capitalization was disregarded in preparing the printed text.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript word division and letter forms
editorial_practices.representation: Word division regularized; i/j and u/v normalized; z printed where the manuscript's yogh represents z
editorial_practices.description: Menner's stated editorial method changes word division and several letter-form distinctions while otherwise aiming to preserve the manuscript text.
notes: The schema's punctuation-normalization category is used broadly here; the recorded interventions extend beyond punctuation.

editorial_practices.representation: Title page and Middle English text transcribed continuously; most introduction, notes, glossary, bibliography, and appendix represented only by page milestones and linked images
editorial_practices.description: U-M's electronic text selects the work and title page from the larger source edition without transcribing most scholarly apparatus.

scope.notes: Matching item identifier, header, text, page sequence, and raw-package XML establish derivation; repository-level additions and the exact transfer process are not documented.

subject.description: Diplomatic edition of the complete Cleanness text

assertion.notes: Olsen reports repeated personal manuscript study and examination of BL high-resolution TIFFs supplied in 2011.

rights.component: Repository XML file and repository-specific additions
rights.basis: The repository file contains a public-domain medieval text and source-edition content, but no file-specific license or complete authorship statement for repository-level XML additions was found in the reviewed artifact.
notes: Do not infer a license for the repository XML solely from the status of the medieval work, Menner's edition, or U-M's current item-level rights statement.

rights.component: U-M electronic text as presented by the current item page
rights.basis: The current U-M item page states that the materials are in the public domain and supplies the RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States label.
notes: This records U-M's current provider assertion. The legacy XML also contains a generic warning that copyright may apply where applicable; the two statements are preserved rather than silently reconciled.

rights.component: U-M page images of Menner's 1920 volume
rights.basis: U-M's item-level statement describes its materials as public domain and labels the item No Copyright–United States; the underlying 1920 volume is also presented as public domain by Internet Archive.

rights.component: 1920 printed edition, including editorial text and apparatus
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1920, and both the current U-M item and Internet Archive record present the digitized material as public domain in the United States.
The Internet Archive metadata assigns Public Domain Mark 1.0 to the item.
notes: The public-domain mark is a status assertion, not a conventional copyright license. Provider platform terms may still apply to access.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1869 and the scan's Google notice describes the underlying book as public domain.
notes: Google's scan notice requests attribution and noncommercial use; this provider request is recorded separately from the underlying book's public-domain copyright status.

rights.basis: The volume was published in 1923; Internet Archive metadata for the reviewed scan reports no visible copyright notice and not-in-copyright status.
notes: This status concerns the 1923 publication in the United States; it does not determine the contractual terms for modern BL-commissioned images.

rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content of Cleanness
rights.basis: The text is an anonymous fourteenth-century work; this statement is confined to the medieval textual content and does not cover modern photographs, metadata, editions, or access contracts.

rights.component: University of Calgary microfilm-derived page images
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms; jurisdiction not stated on item records
rights.basis: Calgary metadata labels the images Public Domain and links Public Domain Mark 1.0, while the same presentation says they are supplied under British Library license for private single-display viewing, limits free use to noncommercial purposes, and enumerates education, instruction, private study, and research.
notes: The public-domain label and restrictive provider/licensor terms conflict. Do not describe these images as unconditionally reusable or commercially redistributable without clarification from Calgary or the British Library.
The exact page-detail rights panel is more specific than collection search facets.

rights.component: Commons-hosted complete PDF compiled from Calgary images
rights.jurisdiction: Wikimedia Commons provider assertion; worldwide mark
rights.basis: Commons applies a mechanical-scan public-domain rationale and Public Domain Mark 1.0 to the hosted PDF.
notes: Confidence is high that Commons makes this assertion, not that Commons resolves Calgary's conflicting BL-license and noncommercial-use terms.
Do not transfer this provider claim to Calgary, the British Library's own images, or newly ordered photography.

rights.component: Images supplied through British Library Imaging Services
rights.basis: Current imaging terms say supplied images must not be copied or reproduced without written permission and that permission or licensing fees may apply.
notes: This contractual reuse condition is separate from the copyright status of the fourteenth-century text and from Commons's claim for its hosted derivative.

rights.component: Olsen's diplomatic edition, introduction, notes, and bibliography
rights.basis: The front matter states copyright in Kenna L. Olsen; no open license was found on the publication page or in the four reviewed PDFs.
notes: Publicly downloadable does not mean licensed for republication.

open_questions.question: May the Commons-hosted complete PDF or Calgary page images be redistributed in this repository or a commercial print workflow despite Calgary's British Library license and noncommercial-use language?
next_steps: Ask the University of Calgary and British Library for written clarification covering repository redistribution, public display, print-on-demand, and commercial and noncommercial uses.
Until clarified, link to the external Commons and Calgary records rather than bundling the cached manuscript PDF in a deployable viewer or publication artifact.
Preserve provider-specific assertions separately; do not treat Commons's Public Domain Mark as permission from Calgary or the British Library.
notes: This is a rights and contract ambiguity, not a statement that the medieval text itself is copyrighted.

open_questions.question: Will the British Library restore a public item viewer or publish a usable IIIF manifest for Cotton MS Nero A X/2?
next_steps: Recheck the exact BL catalog record and historical LARK periodically.
Ask BL Reference Services whether a replacement item viewer or IIIF endpoint exists.
Keep the current unavailable status date-bounded.

open_questions.question: What exact transformation and responsibility chain produced the repository XML from U-M's raw XML?
next_steps: Inspect the CME repository history or transformation scripts for acs0188.
Do not attribute U-M raw-package differences to a named person without evidence.
notes: Observed differences include an XML declaration, NODE identifiers, and a changed EDITORIALDECL level, but the responsible process is undocumented.

review_notes: Work-level title, author, composition, genre, subjects, and literary sources are cataloged in the companion metadata manifest; this file confines itself to textual and reproduction lineage, access, editorial method, and rights evidence.
The BL catalog's Cleanness range modern ff. 60r–86r includes two prefatory illustrations; text proper is modern ff. 61r–86r. Old ink ranges are ff. 56r–82r and 57r–82r respectively.
The complete Commons PDF and both Gollancz scans map the whole physical codex and therefore include structured work_portion locators rather than being mislabeled as work-only files.
